watch_list.why: EO analytics for climate + environment; Charlottesville; commercial data licensing; small team at inflection point.
watch_list.roast: Charlottesville EO startup that's survived the Series A graveyard by being quietly useful rather than pitch-deck shiny. They analyze satellite imagery for climate applications, which is the polite way of saying 'we surveil supply chains for Fortune 500 sustainability officers who need defensible evidence before their ESG report gets subpoenaed.' The product is boring, the market is enormous, and the sales cycle tightens every quarter as European disclosure rules bite down harder. They need a BD lead who can walk a Chief Sustainability Officer through 'yes, we can see what your cotton supplier is doing in Xinjiang' without the meeting ending in legal review. You survived ENI carbon negotiations on two continents. A CSO in Zoom will be a softer opponent.

watch_list.why: Commercial SAR constellation, US-based; acquired by IonQ in August 2025. Next-gen Acadia satellites rolling out late 2026. Active Global Solutions BD team hiring in EMEA. Dual-use commercial + government customer base matches KSAT experience directly.
watch_list.roast: San Francisco SAR shop that got acquired by IonQ last summer in a deal that confused everyone including the acquirers — apparently the plan is to bolt Capella's birds onto a quantum key distribution network, which is either the future of cryptography or a $200M research project that sounded great in a 14th-floor conference room. Either way, the SAR business still runs, the commercial customers still pay, and someone in sales still has to explain to an insurance analyst why an all-weather radar image costs what it costs. They're scaling to next-gen Acadia late 2026, hiring BD in EMEA, and pretending the IonQ narrative doesn't make their own pitch deck a six-page flowchart. Your KSAT dual-use background — Norwegian intelligence in the morning, NOAA scientists by afternoon, both pretending not to see each other in the cafeteria — is exactly the muscle they keep hiring for and losing to quantum-curious recruiters who mistake Bloch spheres for go-to-market. Apply before a physicist with a sales LinkedIn headline beats you to it.

watch_list.why: Amsterdam-based AI + satellite MRV for agroforestry and reforestation. Tracks additionality, biodiversity, and socioeconomic metrics. Direct fit for Paul's carbon + EO + regulatory background. Early-stage with small team — commercial adult likely needed as they scale.
watch_list.roast: Amsterdam team using AI and satellite imagery to predict which trees are about to get chopped down, then selling carbon credits for NOT chopping them — a business model that would make a seventeenth-century Dutch merchant proud and an EU regulator nervous. Their pitch hinges on 'additionality,' the carbon market's way of saying 'prove you actually did something beyond business as usual,' a concept that destroyed half the voluntary carbon market in 2023 when Bloomberg sent flying drones to verify claims that turned out to be vibes. Treecycle's tech actually solves additionality, which means they'll either get bought by Verra for a fortune or get regulated into oblivion by Brussels in roughly that order. Probably both. You wrote actual carbon regulations before most of their team had their first Keurig pod. They'd hire you the same week you applied just so their next due-diligence session becomes survivable. Amsterdam is a shorter flight from Tromsø than half your old KSAT commercial trips. Go send them a 'hoi.'

watch_list.why: Leading carbon registry; digital MRV + satellite verification growing fast.
watch_list.roast: The Vatican of carbon credits — everyone genuflects, nobody fully believes. After years of journalists catching 'offsets' that were just untouched forests they were never going to cut anyway, they're pivoting hard to satellite-based MRV because their old 'trust us, the trees are still there' system stopped working when Bloomberg started flying drones. You literally wrote carbon regulations before most of these people finished grad school. Walking in there would be like a seminarian showing up at Sunday school — overqualified, overexperienced, and exactly the adult supervision they're desperately missing. If they hire outside your resume, it's malpractice.

watch_list.why: Satellite-based carbon + crop monitoring; EUDR compliance angle; commercial data programs.
watch_list.roast: They convinced Wall Street that staring at cornfields from space is a financial product. Carbon credits from agriculture, EUDR compliance, crop monitoring — it's the unholy trinity of 'things that sound boring but generate enormous invoices.' Based in the Midwest, which means your Taos zip code is either a dealbreaker or proof you understand remote work. Their ideal hire is someone who can talk soil carbon sequestration at a farm bureau meeting and not get punched. You ski telemark. You can handle farmers.

watch_list.why: Drone data marketplace brokering aerial + EO data globally; commercial partnerships model.
watch_list.roast: Stockholm marketplace that turned 'hire a drone pilot' into a global SaaS play, which sounds dumb until you realize an enterprise customer needing aerial data across 150 countries doesn't want 150 contractor invoices — they want one login and one receivable. That's the whole business. Uber for flying cameras, but with actual commercial gravity. The hard part is the sales pitch: explaining to a Fortune 500 procurement VP why Globhe beats buying drones in-house when everyone and their cousin now owns a DJI. Your KSAT years were the same song in a bigger airframe — sell the data, abstract away the hardware, collect the PO. The Swedish CEO will serve you better fika than most Series B boards have budget for, and your 'hei' lands softer than whatever McKinsey kid they're currently interviewing. Go send the note.

watch_list.why: IoT + AIS data from LEO constellation; maritime tracking; growing commercial verticals.
watch_list.roast: Spanish company putting LoRa gateways in orbit so shipping companies can track cargo in the one place cell networks go to die — the middle of the Atlantic. Their pitch is 'cell towers, but orbiting,' which is either the dumbest or smartest thing you'll hear this quarter depending on whether you've ever tried to find a signal off Tromso in January. Maritime IoT is so underserved that even a modest constellation prints real revenue, and the customers (shipping, fishing, cold-chain asset tracking) would rather pay a subscription than explain dead cargo to their insurer. Your KSAT background speaks both 'maritime' and 'satellite' fluently, and there are maybe five humans on Earth who can say that without lying. Go be one of them.

watch_list.why: Hyperspectral satellite startup; commercial partnerships for precision agriculture.
watch_list.roast: Indian hyperspectral startup that's pulled roughly $95M total to take pictures of Earth in 150 different colors — which sounds pretentious until you remember every regular RGB satellite missed the methane plume that just cost ExxonMobil another Congressional hearing. Their Firefly constellation is live, commercial contracts are real, and the Bangalore engineering team has the rare combination of 'actually ships hardware' and 'doesn't cost Silicon Valley salaries' that keeps VCs refilling the rounds. The BD challenge is explaining spectral unmixing to a mining executive whose last technical training was on Windows XP without either party losing the will to live. You've negotiated ENI deals in rooms where the CFO couldn't tell SAR from SaaS. Bring your A-game, a translator, and the surgical patience your KSAT years forged through forty winters of Norwegian small talk.

watch_list.why: Satellite-based analytics for water utilities, dams, forests. Series A, UK-based. No BD roles open now but strong domain overlap.
watch_list.roast: British company that tells utility executives their dams, pipes, and pylons are quietly rotting — from orbit — then sends an invoice for the bad news. Infrastructure monitoring via satellite for water utilities and grid operators, which is the business equivalent of being a very expensive hypochondriac doctor who keeps being right. Thames Water just paid a £104M fine for sewage discharge, which means every water utility in Britain now has a compliance line item labeled 'do not let us end up on the Guardian front page.' Rezatec is the firm they call. The commercial pitch is practically cinema: 'Your infrastructure is failing. We have evidence. Pay us before the regulator does.' You survived ENI boardrooms where tea was the only thing keeping the conversation civil. Handling a Severn Trent procurement officer on a Zoom window is Tuesday before lunch.

watch_list.why: Norwegian drone + satellite company from Andoya Space ecosystem. ESA-funded. KSAT-adjacent. Very small, monitor for growth.
watch_list.roast: Your Norwegian ex-colleagues started a drone company in Andøya without telling you. ESA-funded, KSAT-adjacent, and they probably still use the same coffee machine you used to curse at. Emergency response and mapping applications, which means they're the people who show up after a landslide with expensive equipment and good intentions. You could run their commercial side in your sleep, and the fact that you haven't applied yet is either strategic restraint or spite. Both are valid. Go send a 'hei' already.

watch_list.why: Stratospheric balloon remote sensing for wildfire and mapping. Denver-based. No BD roles now, mostly engineering.
watch_list.roast: Denver stratospheric balloon company that maps wildfires from 65,000 feet because apparently drones gave them altitude issues. The product is weirdly great — resolution that sits right between drones and satellites, loitering capacity that eats an aircraft's lunch, and a price point that makes insurance adjusters pay cash on the first call. Colorado fire seasons keep rewriting their own records, which means the commercial team will expand whether Urban Sky wants it to or not. You're in the same time zone, they probably know half your ski crew already, and introducing yourself at parties as 'the balloon intelligence guy' is the kind of origin story Cormac would respect. Watch this one and pounce the moment a BD req goes up.

watch_list.why: Paul would apply for the right role here. Watch for Director/VP of Commercial, Head of BD, VP of Sales, or revenue-leadership openings. PMM (2026-04 posting) is not the seat; rejected for AE 2026-03-31. Comp floor $165K.
watch_list.roast: They bought UrtheCast's leftovers at a discount and are trying to build a daily global imagery empire from the wreckage. It's like buying a foreclosed house and telling everyone you're a real estate developer. BUT — they actually have a constellation, actual data, and actual customers, which puts them ahead of 90% of NewSpace companies that only have a pitch deck and a dream. They need someone to figure out who wants to buy pictures of the entire planet taken every single day. Spoiler: it's everyone. They just don't know it yet. That's your job.

watch_list.why: Heat pump manufacturer, climate-tech darling, DCVC-backed. Director-level sales role surfaced in audit.
watch_list.roast: Venture-backed heat pump company. Let that sink in. Someone looked at an HVAC unit and said 'this could use some disruption and a $50M Series B.' They need a Strategic Sales Director, which is corporate for 'person who convinces contractors to install our fancy heat pumps instead of the ones they've been installing for 30 years.' If you can sell satellite data to Norwegian oil companies in Arctic darkness, you can sell Americans comfortable air. The bar is literally room temperature.

watch_list.why: Swappable battery containers for cargo ships. Ocean + climate crossover. Growth Manager role spotted.
watch_list.roast: They want to electrify cargo ships using swappable battery containers. It's the most 'what if we just... made the ship electric?' idea since someone tried to put sails back on tankers. Maritime decarbonization is your ocean-adjacent KSAT world colliding with climate tech's wildest ambitions. Their 'Growth Manager' title is just 'BD person who owns a hoodie.' You owned hoodies in Tromsø before it was a tech uniform.

watch_list.why: Forest carbon verification via satellite and AI. Your MRV and remote sensing background is a direct hit.
watch_list.roast: Silicon Valley's answer to the rainforest: throw LiDAR at it. They verify carbon credits by staring at trees from orbit, which is basically what you've been accidentally training for your entire career. Well-funded, perpetually hiring, and located in San Francisco where a one-bedroom costs more than a house in Taos. They need BD people who can explain remote sensing to carbon market buyers without the buyer falling asleep, dying of boredom, or both. You've survived Norwegian winter. You can survive a sales call with a carbon trader.

watch_list.why: Building weather and climate satellites. Commercial partnerships team would be a fit.
watch_list.roast: Named after a subatomic particle because 'Boring But Reliable Climate Satellite Company' doesn't fit on a business card — but that IS the pitch and in 2026 it's a winning one. While Starcloud raises $170M to put data centers in orbit and Windborne floats balloon confetti into the stratosphere, Muon just keeps launching working satellites that measure real things — temperature, water vapor, greenhouse gas plumes — and selling the data to NOAA, NASA, and the reinsurance majors who need to model hurricane seasons that keep breaking records. Real hardware, real contracts, engineers who've actually bent metal, in a sector full of PowerPoint constellations. The Venn diagram of 'understands commercial satellite data licensing' AND 'can walk into a reinsurance CFO's office without getting stuck on basic physics' has maybe six humans in it. You're annoyingly one of them. Go send a note before the latest class of Stanford MBAs with zero satellite experience convinces the board that 'climate intelligence' is a marketing problem.

watch_list.why: Thermal infrared satellite data for agriculture and water. Niche EO play with commercial potential.
watch_list.roast: They take the Earth's temperature. From space. That's the company. Thermal infrared for crop water stress, which sounds like the world's most specific WebMD for corn. Every ag company will pay for this in five years but right now they're early enough that joining means you'd BE the commercial team. That's either your dream or your nightmare, and based on the fact that you once built KSAT's commercial operations from scratch in literal Arctic darkness, I'm guessing dream.

watch_list.why: The Moody's of carbon credits. Rates quality of offsets using satellite data. Your MRV background is gold here.
watch_list.roast: The Moody's of carbon credits, except the thing they're rating is whether a tree is actually still standing or if someone just Photoshopped the forest back in. London-based, well-funded, and staffed by people who use the word 'integrity' without irony. Your satellite verification background combined with your actual carbon market experience makes you catnip for these people. Like, embarrassingly perfect. You should probably apply just to see their recruiter's face when they read your resume.

watch_list.why: Satellite-based vegetation management for power utilities. Climate resilience meets boring infrastructure money.
watch_list.roast: Amsterdam-based company that uses satellites to tell power companies which trees are about to commit murder on their power lines. The most boringly genius business model in climate tech: utilities have unlimited budgets for 'please stop California from catching fire,' and Overstory cashes those checks with a straight face. Remote-possible, real recurring revenue, and the kind of company where your EO background meets actual money. Not 'potential TAM' money. Actual checks-clearing money.

watch_list.why: Science-based carbon removal verification. Like Verra but for permanent removal, not offsets.
watch_list.roast: Verra's angry younger sibling who thinks everyone else is too soft on carbon credits. They verify permanent carbon removal — the 'literally buried it underground for a thousand years' kind, not the 'we planted a sapling and called it a day' kind. Stripe and Frontier money keeps the lights on, and their methodology team has more PhDs per square foot than most MIT labs. Your regulatory background — writing actual carbon laws before most of them had an undergrad thesis — makes you the adult in every room they put you in. They desperately need someone commercial who speaks 'science' without flinching. You do.

watch_list.why: The OG daily imagery company. Always hiring. You probably know people there already.
watch_list.roast: The Golden State Warriors of EO — been around forever, won some championships, and every quarterly earnings call features the same analyst asking 'but when will you be profitable?' Stock's been hacking out of a multi-year base while stock-based comp keeps chewing through cash. They still photograph the entire Earth every day and still haven't fully figured out who wants to buy all those pictures — which is exactly the problem a BD leader solves. You've met half their go-to-market team at conferences and pretended to like their booth. Time to stop pretending. They need someone who can actually sell the data, not just rebrand it every eighteen months.

watch_list.why: Above-ground biomass mapping from satellite data. Finnish company, small, very niche.
watch_list.roast: Finnish company that measures tree biomass from space. That's it. That's the whole company. Twelve people in Helsinki staring at forests and calculating carbon. Joining would mean you ARE the commercial team, the marketing team, and probably the person who orders lunch. It's either the most terrifying or most liberating career move you could make, and honestly after 48 rejected applications, 'small Finnish tree-measuring startup' has a certain unhinged appeal.

watch_list.why: Climate risk analytics for assets and supply chains. EO-adjacent, enterprise SaaS.
watch_list.roast: They tell companies which of their buildings will be underwater in 20 years, then charge them for the existential crisis. Climate risk analytics for the 'oh god SEC disclosure requirements are real' crowd. Long sales cycles, fat contracts, and clients who are legally obligated to care about what you're selling. If you can handle a CFO asking 'but how accurate is your flood model, really?' without laughing, crying, or both, you're their person. You've had worse conversations. You lived in Tromsø.

watch_list.why: Constellation of weather balloons collecting atmospheric data. Paul's NewSpace + climate crossover.
watch_list.roast: Khosla-backed startup that launches weather balloons into the stratosphere by the hundreds, like meteorological confetti, while Starcloud just took $170M at a $1.1B valuation to put data centers in orbit and Vantor is minting two new EO constellations in the same quarter. In a year when atmospheric real estate is getting crowded fast, Windborne is quietly printing balloons AND customers — national weather services, defense agencies, reinsurance funds, anyone who needs temperature and pressure where satellites can't see and airplanes won't fly. Not sexy. Not on a magazine cover. Neither is Taos in April mud season when the drought left only seventy-six inches on the slopes and TSV closed a month early. The unglamorous business is often the one still standing in five years. Go see if they need a commercial adult before the Bay Area hires three more ex-McKinsey associates who've never talked to a reinsurance underwriter in their life.

watch_list.why: Drone-based ecosystem restoration and monitoring. Oxford spinout, scaling commercially.
watch_list.roast: Oxford spinout that plants trees with drones and then watches them grow with satellites. It's the full Black Mirror episode where technology saves nature, except it's real and they're hiring. They need someone who can pitch 'we'll restore your ecosystem, sir' to a mining company executive without either of you bursting into flames from the irony. Your ability to keep a straight face during absurd business negotiations was forged in ENI boardrooms. Deploy it here.

watch_list.why: Weather intelligence platform with own radar satellites. Commercial BD team is substantial.
watch_list.roast: Israeli founders, Boston HQ, their own radar satellites, and the audacity to say 'we're going to be the Bloomberg Terminal of weather.' They sell forecasts to everyone from Delta Airlines to the US Air Force, which means their BD team handles calls where the stakes range from 'flight delays' to 'national security.' Your satellite + enterprise sales background would land well, and they're a Patriots-friendly commute if you ever go hybrid. Finally, a company where your Brady obsession is geographically justified.

watch_list.why: Satellite-based ag MRV platform. Soil carbon, sustainability reporting, EUDR. Direct domain overlap.
watch_list.roast: They measure dirt from space and sell the results to food companies who need proof they're not destroying civilization. Soil carbon, sustainability reporting, EUDR compliance — it's the full European bureaucracy bingo card, and every square is a revenue stream. Your carbon market plus satellite background is such a comically perfect fit that their ATS might flag your application as 'too good to be real, probably a bot.' Apply anyway. Make the robot question its algorithms.

watch_list.why: Spanish EO startup, $130M Series B (Apr 2026) led by Nazca Capital. Seven years in stealth, moving to commercialization. L3Harris sensor partnership. Direct match for commercial EO data licensing leadership.
watch_list.roast: Spanish startup that just took $130M from Nazca Capital in April 2026 to map Earth for AI. Their pitch: 'what if satellites, but the data trains ChatGPT's cousin?' Seven years in stealth, now shoveling pictures of the planet into neural nets. L3Harris is building the sensors, which means grown-ups signed the check. You understand commercial EO data licensing in ways that four separate generations of VC darlings do not, and they just got enough money to actually hire someone who knows what they're doing. That's you. Apply before they hire a former McKinsey consultant who's never licensed a pixel in their life.

watch_list.why: Paris-based EO analytics for energy, methane, and climate markets. Sells to hedge funds, oil majors, governments. ENI-era Paul speaks this customer's language fluently.
watch_list.roast: Paris-based satellite data company that got famous catching Russian methane leaks from space and selling the footage to hedge funds. Their product pitch is 'environmental intelligence,' which is the business equivalent of being the DJ at a very expensive party where oil majors, sovereign wealth funds, and climate activists all pretend not to know each other. Your ENI days were basically the same room — European regulatory sophistication, corporate energy executives, satellite vendor in the corner closing the deal. You were that vendor. You speak their language, you survived French bureaucracy once, and you know which side of the methane ledger actually pays. Send a 'bonjour.'

watch_list.why: SF-based hyperspectral EO constellation for pipeline and infrastructure monitoring. Defense + commercial energy customers. BD role overlap with Paul's dual-use commercial experience.
watch_list.roast: California hyperspectral startup that stares at pipelines for a living. They detect methane leaks and pipeline corrosion from orbit, which is somehow less creepy than the Russian surveillance states but equally commercial. Backed by real DoD contracts and commercial energy majors, with a tech stack that reads like a Transformer's cousin. They need BD help to navigate between defense work and oil & gas deals without either customer flinching — which is exactly the split you learned to handle at KSAT when your Arctic ground stations served NOAA in the morning and the Norwegian military by afternoon. You can already talk 'monitoring' to an ExxonMobil VP without breaking eye contact. Go do it.

watch_list.why: Munich-based thermal infrared satellite constellation; 180+ employees; wildfire detection + climate intelligence; actively hiring commercial roles. Promoted distributed-sat pioneer Ignacio Zuleta to CTPO in Feb 2026. Direct KSAT-adjacent culture; European climate-tech customers.
watch_list.roast: German thermal intelligence company with 180 engineers staring at the Earth's temperature from space, which sounds like a niche hobby until you remember wildfires are a thirty-billion-dollar insurance problem and they spot ignitions before the fire department's coffee gets cold. They just elevated a distributed-satellite pioneer to CTPO in February, which is Silicon Valley code for 'grown-ups are in the product meetings now and the commercial side is next.' Munich is close enough to Tromsø that your Norwegian LinkedIn graph overlaps theirs by three degrees and a shared appreciation for dark beer. Thermal IR is the one EO niche where the customer — insurers, utilities, emergency managers — literally cannot say 'no' when their asset is on fire. Selling to people whose alternative is 'watch it burn' is the easiest commercial pitch of your career. Pronounce it Oh-ROH-rah-Tek and skip the line.

watch_list.why: Finnish SAR empire scaling to 50 sats/year by end-2026. NGA + NRO contracts, insurance + maritime + flood commercial side. Dual-use muscle Paul built at KSAT directly applies. Helsinki HQ, US BD in Virginia.
watch_list.roast: Finnish SAR empire that got rich selling radar pictures to the NRO, NGA, and every European intelligence agency with a checkbook. They're scaling to fifty satellites a year by end of 2026, which is the NewSpace equivalent of Henry Ford opening River Rouge — except the Model Ts see through clouds and cost forty million apiece. The commercial side is the part still looking for a BD adult: selling the same constellation to insurance giants, hedge funds, and flood-mapping customers who don't want to be in the same PowerPoint as a defense contract. That dual-use-without-freaking-out-civilians muscle is exactly what you built at KSAT, where NOAA scientists and Norwegian intelligence analysts shared a ground station and politely pretended not to see each other in the cafeteria. Helsinki is a short flight from Tromsø and they probably still have your old coffee order memorized.

watch_list.why: Belgian EO satellite manufacturer opening Megafactory (world's 3rd largest) in 2026 with new US manufacturing facility. Vertically integrated: builds constellation + sells imagery. US expansion means a US commercial leadership org is inevitable. Early-mover window for BD leadership.
watch_list.roast: Belgian satellite manufacturer opening a Megafactory in the US because Europe apparently couldn't contain their ambition. They build their own birds and sell the imagery, which makes them a rare vertically integrated player in a market otherwise crowded with 'we rent pixels from someone else' middlemen. Brussels bureaucracy forged them, which means they survived the same kind of byzantine EU procurement you endured at ENI — you two will have a lot to share over the sad conference coffee. The US expansion guarantees a US commercial org is coming, and whoever lands that seat gets to define the go-to-market for a constellation that isn't fully online yet. First-mover energy. Apply before some McKinsey alum with a French MBA and zero satellite data experience convinces the board otherwise.

watch_list.why: Finnish hyperspectral EO constellation; dual-use commercial + defense customers; ESA-aligned; KSAT-ecosystem adjacent. Commercial BD leadership window as constellation scales and CHIME-era European hyperspectral market comes online.
watch_list.roast: Finnish hyperspectral startup doing the thing hyperspectral startups do — selling 150 colors of Earth to whoever will listen, which turns out to be defense agencies, precision ag analytics firms, and the occasional hedge fund that wants to short soybeans three quarters early. The advantage of being Finnish is that the team ships actual product in temperatures that would make a Palo Alto founder write a Medium post about burnout, and they survived European commercial procurement without developing the learned helplessness most EU SaaS companies fall into. Your Tromsø-KSAT years speak fluent Nordic commercial, and there are maybe eight humans alive who can explain spectral unmixing to a mining executive without either party falling asleep. You're one of them. ESA's CHIME mission is about to blow open the European hyperspectral TAM, and Kuva is positioning as the commercial backbone. Watch for the US BD lead req when they expand this side of the Atlantic.

watch_list.why: Public NYSE:SPIR. 100+ sat constellation collecting weather, maritime (AIS), aviation (ADS-B). Global commercial org across Virginia/SF/Glasgow/Singapore. BD turnover is steady; KSAT-adjacent multi-vertical commercial muscle directly applies.
watch_list.roast: Public company with a stock chart that looks like a telemark wipeout on a sheet-ice day, except underneath the broken IPO and the Yahoo Finance comments section is an actual working global constellation of 100+ birds pulling weather, AIS, and ADS-B data that airlines, insurers, and navies keep paying for despite the equity narrative. Headquarters are in Virginia AND San Francisco AND Glasgow AND Singapore, which is code for 'we're somehow both a US defense contractor and a European climate shop,' which is the exact KSAT duality you spent a decade running. They're always hiring BD — turnover is Silicon Valley standard, the data is Brussels-class, and somebody has to sell forecasts to Delta while the engineers keep launching cubesats. Your satellite + enterprise background plus the fact that you'll actually take a 6am Oslo call makes you an unusually clean fit. The stock might still go to zero. The business might not. Those are two separate questions and you only need to care about the second one.
